Q50 BRUTAL
ETL pipeline all generators source→transform→sink.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in generators by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: ETL pipeline all generators source→transform→sink. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Infinite Iterator Algebra Library
Your goal:

Build itertools-like generators from scratch — lazy evaluation mastery.

What you must do:

Implement: count(start, step), cycle(iterable), repeat(obj, n), chain(*iters), islice(iter, stop), takewhile, dropwhile, groupby(sorted_iter, key), tee(iter, n=2) with buffered replay. Each must be generator-based lazy. Demo: infinite prime stream via filter+count using your primitives only. Compare memory itertools vs yours on infinite take 1000.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No itertools in implementations.
  • tee buffers shared correctly when advanced unevenly.
  • groupby requires sorted input documented.
  • 22+ tests vs itertools reference.
Done when: First 100 primes match known list; tee replays independently.
Q52 MEGA
Generator-Based Coroutine Pipeline (yield send)
Your goal:

Classic coroutine pattern before async/await.

What you must do:

Build pipeline: read_lines(path) -> grep(pattern) -> count -> sink. Each stage is generator accepting send() for config updates mid-stream. grep accepts send('pattern=new') to change filter live. Process 1GB file simulated with generator of lines. Close() propagates GeneratorExit cleanly.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • At least 4 pipeline stages.
  • send updates pattern without restart.
  • GeneratorExit handled no ResourceWarning.
  • Memory O(1) vs file size.
Done when: Mid-stream pattern change affects subsequent lines only; pipeline completes.
Q53 MEGA
Custom Iterable & Iterator Protocol Classes
Your goal:

Implement both __iter__ and __next__ patterns correctly.

What you must do:

RangeLike(start, stop, step) as iterator class. BinaryTree in-order Iterable wrapper separate from Node. PermutationIterator without materializing all perms. FlattenIterable depth-first over nested list/dict structure. All work with for-loops and list() but lazy by default.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Separate iterable vs iterator where appropriate.
  • StopIteration not swallowed incorrectly.
  • Flatten handles arbitrary nesting depth.
  • 18+ protocol tests.
Done when: for x in RangeLike(0,10,2) yields 0,2,4,6,8; tree inorder sorted.
Q54 MEGA
Backpressure Streaming CSV Aggregator
Your goal:

Producer-consumer with bounded queue between generators.

What you must do:

Producer generator reads chunks from file. Consumer generator aggregates stats per chunk. Bounded buffer using collections.deque maxlen or queue.Queue size. Slow consumer triggers producer pause (pull-based: consumer drives). Report throughput rows/sec and max buffer occupancy.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Pull model: consumer calls next on producer.
  • Max buffer size configurable.
  • Handle consumer early stop closing producer.
  • Benchmark 5M rows.
Done when: Buffer never exceeds max; early break closes file handle.
Q55 MEGA
Resumable Generator Checkpoint System
Your goal:

Save and restore generator state for long jobs.

What you must do:

Serialize generator state: name, locals, instruction position approximated via step count. For simple generator with yield in loop, implement checkpoint every N yields. Resume continues from saved yield count (simulate by re-instantiate and skip). Apply to Monte Carlo simulation 1M iterations resumable after crash. Document limits vs full frame pickle.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Checkpoint file JSON with progress counter.
  • Resume idempotent.
  • Monte Carlo partial sum matches non-resumed within stats.
  • 10+ checkpoint tests.
Done when: Kill at 40% resume completes same total as uninterrupted run.
Q56 MEGA
Async-Generator-Like Sync: yield from Tree Walk
Your goal:

yield from delegation for tree and graph traversal.

What you must do:

walk_tree(root) yields nodes depth-first using yield from on children. walk_graph(start, adj) yields nodes BFS/DFS selectable. Subgraph filter generator skips branches matching predicate. Compare manual recursion vs yield from stack depth. Count nodes without building full list.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • yield from used in tree walk.
  • Cycle-safe graph walk.
  • Filter prune skips entire subtrees.
  • 15+ tests on known trees.
Done when: Node count via sum(1 for _ in walk) matches manual count.
Q57 MEGA
Windowed & Rolling Statistics Generators
Your goal:

Streaming analytics with fixed memory windows.

What you must do:

rolling(iterable, n) yields tuples of last n items. rolling_mean, rolling_std, rolling_max generators. Apply to synthetic sensor stream 1M readings. EWM exponential weighted mean generator with alpha param. Export last 100 windows to CSV.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • O(1) update per step for mean using running sum.
  • Std optional O(n) window recompute OK for clarity.
  • 1M stream without storing 1M windows list.
  • Verify against pandas rolling on sample (pandas test only).
Done when: rolling_mean matches pandas on 500 points; 1M stream completes.
Q58 MEGA
Parser Combinator Generator Library
Your goal:

Yield partial parse results — functional parsing style.

What you must do:

Primitive parsers: literal, regex, seq, choice, many, optional. Return (value, remainder) or ParseError. Parse JSON subset or arithmetic expressions. All parsing lazy where choice backtracks. 20 grammar test cases.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • many(parser) yields list without infinite loop on empty.
  • choice tries alternatives left-to-right.
  • Error includes farthest failure position.
  • 200+ lines.
Done when: Parses nested arithmetic; choice backtracks correctly.
Q59 MEGA
Generator-Based Expander for Template Includes
Your goal:

Lazy expansion of nested includes without full string materialize.

What you must do:

Template syntax: {{ include 'partial.txt' }} and {{ yield section }}. Expander generator yields text chunks lazily. Detect include cycles and raise. Depth limit configurable. Stream expanded output to stdout while reading minimal files.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cycle detection via stack of include paths.
  • Chunks yielded not one giant str.
  • Relative include paths resolved from parent file dir.
  • 12+ include tests.
Done when: Deep include chain streams output; cycle raises IncludeCycleError.
Q60 MEGA
Iterator Cleanup With contextlib.closing Patterns
Your goal:

Resource management in generator-based I/O.

What you must do:

open_lines(path) generator opens file, yields lines, closes in finally. open_lines_ctx manager version using @contextmanager. Multi-file chain generator merging sorted files k-way (heap). Ensure close on garbage collection without leak using weakref finalizer demo. Test leak with tempfile count or mock close counter.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Generator finally runs on close().
  • K-way merge lazy using heapq.
  • Close called exactly once.
  • 15+ resource tests.
Done when: Broken loop still closes file; k-way merge order correct on 3 files.

12 — Context Managers & Exceptions

Topic Map: with statement → Context manager protocol → contextlib → Exception hierarchy → try/except/else/finally → Exception chaining

1. Context Managers — with Statement

Guarantees setup and teardown (cleanup) even if exception occurs.

with open("file.txt") as f:
    data = f.read()
# file automatically closed here, even on exception

1.1 Context manager protocol

class ManagedResource:
    def __enter__(self):
        self.resource = acquire()
        return self.resource
    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        release(self.resource)
        return False  # False = don't suppress exception; True = suppress

with ManagedResource() as r:
    use(r)

2. contextlib.contextmanager

from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def temp_file(path):
    f = open(path, 'w')
    try:
        yield f
    finally:
        f.close()
        os.remove(path)

3. contextlib Utilities

from contextlib import suppress, ExitStack

with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    os.remove("maybe_missing.txt")

with ExitStack() as stack:
    f1 = stack.enter_context(open("a.txt"))
    f2 = stack.enter_context(open("b.txt"))
    # both closed on exit

4. Exception Hierarchy

BaseException
 ├── SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, GeneratorExit
 └── Exception
      ├── ValueError, TypeError, KeyError, IndexError
      ├── AttributeError, RuntimeError, OSError
      └── ... (always catch specific exceptions)

5. try / except / else / finally

try:
    result = risky_operation()
except ValueError as e:
    handle_value_error(e)
except (TypeError, KeyError) as e:
    handle_multiple(e)
else:
    # runs ONLY if no exception in try
    process(result)
finally:
    # ALWAYS runs — cleanup
    cleanup()

5.1 Execution order rules

  • else skipped if except ran
  • finally runs before exception propagates
  • return in try — finally still runs (and can override return!)

6. Exception Chaining

try:
    int("not a number")
except ValueError as e:
    raise RuntimeError("parse failed") from e  # explicit chain
    # raise RuntimeError(...) from None  # suppress context

7. Custom Exceptions

class AppError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, message, code):
        super().__init__(message)
        self.code = code

class ValidationError(AppError):
    pass

8. EAFP vs LBYL

StylePatternWhen
EAFPtry/exceptPythonic default; race conditions
LBYLif check firstExpensive exceptions, hot paths
Gotchas:
  • Bare except: catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt
  • except Exception: still too broad in most cases
  • Swallowing exceptions silently — always log or re-raise
  • __exit__ returning True suppresses exception
How to think about this topic: with guarantees cleanup: __enter__ setup, __exit__ teardown even on exception. Exceptions propagate up until caught; raise ... from e preserves cause chain. EAFP (try/except) is Pythonic when failure is exceptional; LBYL (if check) when check is cheap.

try/except/else/finally execution order

  1. try runs
  2. except if matching exception
  3. else if NO exception in try
  4. finally ALWAYS (even on return)

Connects forward

→ Decorators: @contextmanager. → Async: async with. → Production: custom error hierarchies.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Context managers = structured resource management.
  2. Catch specific exceptions; use else for success path.
  3. raise ... from e preserves debugging context.
  4. Build custom exception hierarchies for your apps.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: generators

Q1 HARD
Context manager timer class.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in context exceptions by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Context manager timer class. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Q50 BRUTAL
Mini app error middleware logging+retry+rollback.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in context exceptions by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Mini app error middleware logging+retry+rollback. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Exception Hierarchy & Error Handling Framework
Your goal:

Design application error taxonomy and centralized handling.

What you must do:

Define AppError base with code, message, details dict, HTTP status mapping. Subtypes: ValidationError, NotFoundError, ConflictError, ExternalServiceError. ErrorHandler registry maps type -> handler returning structured response dict. Context manager suppress() variant that logs instead of silence. CLI app demonstrating catch, wrap, re-raise with __cause__ chain preserved.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Each error serializes to JSON.
  • Handler chain walks MRO.
  • raise X from Y preserves chain in logs.
  • 20+ tests on handling paths.
Done when: Wrapped external error shows __cause__ in log; handler picks correct status.
Q52 MEGA
Retry Context Manager With Exponential Backoff
Your goal:

Context-based retry distinct from decorator approach.

What you must do:

with retry_on(OperationalError, tries=5, base=0.5, max_delay=30): block. On exception matching type, sleep backoff and retry block from start. Optional jitter. Track attempt count exposed as retry_ctx.attempt. Apply to simulated DB commit failing first 3 times.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Non-matching exceptions propagate immediately.
  • Exhaustion raises RetryError wrapping last exception.
  • Context manager implements __enter__/__exit__ correctly.
  • 12+ timing tests with mock sleep.
Done when: Succeeds on 4th attempt; RetryError after max tries.
Q53 MEGA
Nested Context Manager Stack (ExitStack From Scratch)
Your goal:

Replicate contextlib.ExitStack behavior manually.

What you must do:

MyExitStack: enter_context(cm), push(callback), close() LIFO. Suppress exceptions per callback return True/False. Demo: manage 3 temp files, 2 locks, 1 timer all closed on error mid-block. Compare to contextlib.ExitStack on same scenario.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • LIFO exit order verified.
  • Exception in inner block still closes outer resources.
  • push callback receives exc_type/value/tb.
  • 15+ tests.
Done when: All mocks close on exception; order reverse of enter.
Q54 MEGA
Database Transaction Context (Mock) With Savepoints
Your goal:

ACID semantics simulation using context managers.

What you must do:

Connection.transaction() commits on clean exit, rollbacks on exception. Nested savepoint() contexts partial rollback. Log all SQL executed in transaction. Integration test: transfer money both fail and succeed paths. Read-only transaction mode rejects writes.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Mock connection tracks in-memory tables.
  • Savepoint release on success nested.
  • Top-level rollback undoes all nested if outer fails.
  • 18+ transaction tests.
Done when: Failure in nested savepoint rolls back nested only; outer failure rolls back all.
Q55 MEGA
Custom Exception Hook & Traceback Formatter
Your goal:

sys.excepthook replacement for structured logging.

What you must do:

Install custom excepthook writing JSON log: type, message, traceback frames list, locals optional. Redact keys matching password, token, secret in locals. Restore previous hook on shutdown via context manager. Demo uncaught exception in thread (threading.excepthook 3.8+ if available).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Frame list includes filename, lineno, function.
  • Redaction tested on fake password local.
  • Context manager restores old hook.
  • Valid JSON each line.
Done when: Uncaught ValueError logs JSON; password local shows [REDACTED].
Q56 MEGA
Timeout Context Using Signals (Unix) or Thread (Windows)
Your goal:

Cross-platform timeout patterns with cleanup.

What you must do:

timeout(seconds) context raises TimeoutError if block exceeds limit. Document signal SIGALRM approach vs threading.Timer approach. Pick strategy per platform with fallback. Ensure cleanup cancels timer on normal exit. Test on long sleep mock.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Normal completion no TimeoutError.
  • Timer cancelled in __exit__.
  • Platform branch documented.
  • 8+ tests with patched sleep.
Done when: Block exceeding limit raises TimeoutError; fast block exits clean.
Q57 MEGA
Chained Exception Analyzer Tool
Your goal:

Walk __cause__ and __context__ chains for diagnostics.

What you must do:

Given exception instance or traceback, print chain tree: Direct cause vs implicit context labeled. Detect cycles in chain (shouldn't happen but guard). CLI reads log file with serialized exceptions (pickle/json custom). Suggest root cause leaf exception.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Distinguish raise A from B vs bare raise during B handling.
  • ASCII tree output.
  • 5 chained examples in tests.
  • Root cause picks deepest cause when present.
Done when: Tree matches manual chain for nested API failure scenario.
Q58 MEGA
Resource Pool Context Manager
Your goal:

Acquire-release pattern with pool exhaustion handling.

What you must do:

Pool(max=5) of expensive Connection mocks. with pool.acquire(timeout=2) as conn: use conn. Return to pool on exit; broken conn flag removes from pool. Wait queue when pool empty until timeout PoolExhaustedError. Metrics: active, idle, created_total.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Acquire blocks up to timeout.
  • Broken conn not returned to idle pool.
  • Context __exit__ always releases unless broken.
  • Concurrent test 10 threads.
Done when: 6th concurrent acquire waits or times out; pool size never exceeds max.
Q59 MEGA
Warning Filters & Context localwarnings
Your goal:

warnings.catch_warnings advanced usage.

What you must do:

Implement warnings_context(action, category) replicating catch_warnings. Decorator warns_as_errors(category) turning warnings to exceptions. Log all warnings in block to list attachable to logger. Deprecate internal API and test migration path.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • warnings.simplefilter and filterwarnings used correctly.
  • Decorator restores filter on exit.
  • Capture DeprecationWarning in tests.
  • 10+ warning scenarios.
Done when: warns_as_errors raises on DeprecationWarning; context restores after.
Q60 MEGA
Structured Finally: Cleanup Registry Pattern
Your goal:

Alternative to nested try/finally for many cleanups.

What you must do:

CleanupRegistry: register(func, *args), run_all() reverse order. Context manager wraps registry; always runs on exit. Register suppress exceptions per cleanup. Refactor 80-line nested try/finally file into registry style. Compare readability and guarantee same behavior.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • run_all executes reverse registration order.
  • Failure in cleanup logged, continues others.
  • Refactored code passes original tests unchanged.
  • Original and refactored behavior diff zero.
Done when: Registry runs 5 cleanups on exception; refactored tests pass.

13 — Modules, Packages & Project Structure

Topic Map: Import system → Packages → src layout → pyproject.toml → venv → __all__ → Circular imports → Publishing

1. Module Basics

A module is any .py file. A package is a directory with __init__.py (or namespace package without it).

import mymodule              # loads mymodule.py, binds name
from mypackage import utils   # loads mypackage/utils.py
from mypackage.utils import helper
import mypackage.utils as u

2. Import Mechanics

  1. Search sys.path (script dir, PYTHONPATH, site-packages)
  2. Load module once — cached in sys.modules
  3. Execute module top-to-bottom on first import
  4. Bind name in importer's namespace
import importlib
importlib.import_module("mypackage.submodule")
importlib.reload(module)  # re-execute — use carefully

3. Relative vs Absolute Imports

# Absolute (preferred)
from mypackage.core import Engine

# Relative (inside package only)
from . import sibling_module
from ..parent import something
from .subpackage import helper

4. __name__ and __main__

# utils.py
def helper(): ...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # runs only when: python utils.py
    # NOT when: import utils
    helper()
# Run as module (uses package context):
python -m mypackage.cli

5. Recommended Project Structure (src layout)

myproject/
├── pyproject.toml          # build config, dependencies, metadata
├── README.md
├── src/
│   └── mypackage/
│       ├── __init__.py     # public API exports
│       ├── core.py
│       └── utils/
│           ├── __init__.py
│           └── helpers.py
├── tests/
│   ├── conftest.py
│   └── test_core.py
└── .gitignore

6. __init__.py Responsibilities

# mypackage/__init__.py
from .core import Engine, Config
from .utils.helpers import format_output

__all__ = ["Engine", "Config", "format_output"]
__version__ = "1.0.0"

7. Namespace Packages (PEP 420)

Directories without __init__.py can be namespace packages — split across multiple paths. Used for plugin ecosystems.

8. pyproject.toml Essentials

[project]
name = "mypackage"
version = "1.0.0"
dependencies = ["requests>=2.28"]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = ["pytest", "mypy"]

[project.scripts]
mycli = "mypackage.cli:main"

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]

9. Virtual Environments

python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate      # Windows
pip install -e ".[dev]"      # editable install with dev deps

10. Circular Import Solutions

  • Restructure — extract shared code to third module
  • Import inside function (lazy import)
  • Import module, not names: import pkg.mod; pkg.mod.func()
  • Type-checking only: if TYPE_CHECKING: from ... import ...
Gotchas:
  • Shadowing stdlib names (json.py, random.py) breaks imports
  • Relative imports fail when running file directly
  • from pkg import * only exports __all__
  • Mutable state in module scope is shared across entire program
How to think about this topic: Import = find module, execute it once, cache in sys.modules, bind name in your namespace. Packages group modules; __init__.py defines public API. src layout keeps import paths clean. Circular imports = two modules each importing the other at top level — fix with structure or lazy import.

Common blockers

  • ImportError on relative import → run as module: python -m pkg.main
  • Shadowing stdlib → never name file json.py, random.py, etc.
  • Mutable module globals → shared state across entire program.

Connects forward

→ Real projects need pyproject.toml + venv. → Q51+ MEGA: production package layout.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Use src layout + pyproject.toml for real projects.
  2. Understand import cache (sys.modules) and circular import fixes.
  3. python -m package for correct package context.
  4. Editable install (pip install -e .) for development workflow.

Q50 BRUTAL
Production package template full tree+tests+CI config.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in modules by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Production package template full tree+tests+CI config. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Multi-Package Monorepo Layout From Scratch
Your goal:

Design installable package with src layout, CLI entry point, tests.

What you must do:

Create project: src/mypkg/ with submodules core, utils, cli. pyproject.toml with setuptools build, console_scripts entry point. Editable install pip install -e . verification. PHASE 1 — Package exports __version__ from importlib.metadata. PHASE 2 — CLI mypkg greet --name. PHASE 3 — Tests in tests/ with pytest discovery. PHASE 4 — Document public vs private API with __all__.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • src layout not flat package in root.
  • Entry point callable from shell after install.
  • __all__ controls from mypkg import *.
  • README install section accurate.
Done when: pip install -e . then mypkg --help works; tests pass.
Q52 MEGA
Dynamic Plugin Loader With importlib
Your goal:

Runtime module discovery — extensible app pattern.

What you must do:

plugins/ package with modules implementing Plugin protocol. discover_plugins() uses importlib.util, pkgutil.iter_modules. Load by name without importing all at startup (lazy). Handle ImportError in one plugin without breaking others. CLI --list-plugins and --run name args.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No hardcoded plugin name list.
  • Plugin metadata via module __plugin__ dict or class.
  • Broken plugin skipped with warning logged.
  • 15+ discovery tests.
Done when: Add plugin file without editing core; appears in list after rescan.
Q53 MEGA
Configuration Module Cascade (defaults < env < local)
Your goal:

Split settings across modules with explicit precedence.

What you must do:

Package settings: defaults.py, development.py, production.py, local.py optional gitignored. settings/__init__.py merges based on APP_ENV env var. Expose single Config object read-only. Validate required keys at import time with clear error. Document override chain diagram.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • local.py overrides production overrides defaults.
  • Missing required key fails fast at startup.
  • Config immutable after load.
  • 12+ merge tests.
Done when: APP_ENV=development picks dev DB URL; local.py overrides if present.
Q54 MEGA
Circular Import Refactor Workshop
Your goal:

Fix circular imports using TYPE_CHECKING and lazy imports.

What you must do:

Start from broken project: models imports services imports models. Refactor to: protocols in typing module, lazy import inside functions, if TYPE_CHECKING imports for hints only. Verify mypy passes and runtime imports succeed. Write before/after import graph ASCII.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Provide initial broken codebase in fixtures/broken/.
  • Fixed code in src/fixed/.
  • mypy clean on fixed version.
  • Document 3 techniques used.
Done when: python -c 'import fixed.models' no CircularImportError; mypy succeeds.
Q55 MEGA
Namespace Package Simulation
Your goal:

Understand PEP 420 namespace packages.

What you must do:

Two separate directories on sys.path both contribute to namespace pkg plugins. Each adds module without __init__.py in pkg (namespace). Third regular package pkg.core with __init__.py. Demonstrate from pkg.plugins import a works from either path. Explain in module docstring vs regular packages.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Two path entries both provide pkg.plugins.*.
  • No pkg/__init__.py for namespace portion.
  • Demo script modifies sys.path at runtime.
  • Written explanation >= 300 words.
Done when: Modules from two dirs import under same namespace; core remains regular.
Q56 MEGA
Import Hook for Source Encryption Demo (Educational)
Your goal:

Meta_path hook loading bytecode or encrypted source.

What you must do:

Encrypt .py files with simple XOR key; store as .pyenc. Custom import hook decrypts and exec_module into module dict. Register hook on MetaPathFinder. Security disclaimer: obfuscation not security. Round-trip import encrypted module function.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Uses importlib.abc MetaPathFinder and Loader.
  • Normal imports unaffected for unencrypted modules.
  • Key from env var not hardcoded in repo.
  • 10+ import tests.
Done when: from secure import secret_func works via hook; plaintext not on disk.
Q57 MEGA
Version Pinning & Lock File Generator
Your goal:

Parse requirements and resolve tree documentation.

What you must do:

Read requirements.in with ranges; pip freeze simulation or manual resolver for small tree. Output requirements.lock exact pins. Detect conflicts: pkg A needs B<2, pkg C needs B>=2. CLI: lock, check, explain pkg_name dependency chain.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • explain shows why pkg installed (parent chain).
  • Conflict raises with both constraint sources.
  • Lock reproducible on second run.
  • 15+ tests on toy dependency graphs.
Done when: Conflict detected on fixture; explain traces root to leaf.
Q58 MEGA
Module Reload & Hot-Reload Dev Server Lite
Your goal:

importlib.reload semantics and pitfalls.

What you must do:

Watch .py files mtime; on change importlib.reload(module). Track old vs new function identity demo. State preserved in module-level CACHE dict across reload. HTTP server mock routing to reloaded handler function. Document what reload does not refresh (existing instances).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Watchdog or polling loop.
  • Reload only changed module not entire tree.
  • Demo handler output changes after edit without process restart.
  • Warning doc on instances holding old methods.
Done when: Edit handler return string; next request sees new behavior.
Q59 MEGA
Structured Logging Package With Submodule Facades
Your goal:

Package organization: facade pattern over internal modules.

What you must do:

Internal: _formatters.py, _handlers.py, _config.py. Public logging_facade/__init__.py exposes get_logger, configure. Subscribers: json formatter, rotating file handler stub. configure() reads dict config validates keys. Tests import only public API paths.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Leading underscore modules not in __all__.
  • get_logger('name') returns consistent hierarchy.
  • configure idempotent.
  • 20+ logging output tests.
Done when: Third party imports only logging_facade; internal paths not required.
Q60 MEGA
Build & Publish Pipeline Script
Your goal:

End-to-end packaging automation without CI.

What you must do:

Script build.py: bump version in pyproject, run python -m build, check twine metadata. Generate sdist and wheel into dist/. Verify wheel contents list expected package files. Optional TestPyPI upload dry-run documented. Changelog fragment appended from git log since last tag.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Uses build module or setuptools invocation.
  • Version bump semver patch argument.
  • Wheel inspect lists mypkg files.
  • Fail if MANIFEST omits data file.
Done when: build produces installable wheel; pip install wheel.tar works locally.

14 — Type Hints & Static Analysis

Topic Map: Basic hints → Generics → Protocol → TypedDict → Union/Optional → mypy → Runtime validation → Gradual typing strategy

1. Why Type Hints Exist — Two Audiences

Type hints serve you (readable contracts), your IDE (autocomplete, inline errors), and mypy/pyright (batch bug finding). Python at runtime ignores them unless you add a runtime checker (beartype, pydantic).

def withdraw(balance: float, amount: float) -> float:
    if amount > balance:
        raise ValueError("insufficient funds")
    return balance - amount

# mypy catches: withdraw("100", 50)  # error: str not float

2. Modern Syntax (3.10+)

# Union
def parse(s: str) -> int | None: ...    # instead of Optional[int]
def handle(x: int | str) -> None: ...   # instead of Union[int, str]

# Built-in generics (PEP 585)
list[str], dict[str, int], tuple[int, ...], set[float]

3. typing Module Essentials

from typing import (
    Any, Callable, TypeVar, Generic, Protocol,
    Literal, Final, ClassVar, Annotated, TypeAlias,
    TypedDict, NamedTuple, overload, cast
)
from collections.abc import Iterable, Sequence, Mapping, Callable as AbcCallable

4. Generics

T = TypeVar('T')

class Stack(Generic[T]):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._items: list[T] = []
    def push(self, item: T) -> None:
        self._items.append(item)
    def pop(self) -> T:
        return self._items.pop()

s: Stack[int] = Stack()
s.push(42)

5. Protocol — Structural Subtyping

class Drawable(Protocol):
    def draw(self) -> None: ...

def render(obj: Drawable) -> None:
    obj.draw()   # any object with draw() works — no inheritance needed

6. TypedDict

class UserDict(TypedDict):
    name: str
    age: int
    email: str | None

def create_user(data: UserDict) -> None: ...

7. Callable, Literal, Final

Handler = Callable[[int, str], bool]

Mode = Literal["read", "write", "append"]

MAX_SIZE: Final = 1024   # cannot reassign

8. Static Analysis Workflow

# Install
pip install mypy

# Run
mypy src/
mypy --strict src/   # maximum checking

9. Common mypy Patterns

# Type narrowing
def process(x: int | str):
    if isinstance(x, int):
        print(x + 1)   # mypy knows x is int
    else:
        print(x.upper())  # mypy knows x is str

# Assert for narrowing
assert val is not None
val.do_something()  # mypy knows not None

# cast when you know better than mypy
from typing import cast
result = cast(int, maybe_int)

10. Gradual Typing Strategy

  1. Add types to public API first
  2. Use Any sparingly — mark TODO to refine
  3. if TYPE_CHECKING: for import-only types (avoid circular imports)
  4. Enable mypy in CI incrementally
Gotchas:
  • Hints not enforced at runtime — use beartype or pydantic if needed
  • list vs List — prefer lowercase builtins in 3.9+
  • Generic erasure — isinstance(x, list[str]) invalid at runtime
  • Optional[X] means X | None — None must be handled
How to think about this topic: Types are documentation for tools (mypy, IDE), not runtime guards by default. Read hints as contracts: "callers must pass X, I promise to return Y." Use narrowing (isinstance, assert) to refine types in branches. Protocol = "anything that quacks like this" without inheritance.

When hints help most

  • Public API boundaries (functions others call)
  • Complex nested structures (TypedDict, nested generics)
  • Catching None-related bugs (Optional / X | None)

Common blockers

  • mypy errors confusing → read as "contract violated" not "Python broken."
  • Any everywhere → defeats purpose; narrow gradually.
  • Runtime isinstance on Generic → erased; won't work like Java.

Connects forward

→ Async: Awaitable, Coroutine types. → OOP: Protocol vs ABC.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Type hints are for humans and static tools — not runtime enforcement.
  2. Generics + Protocol enable reusable typed libraries.
  3. Run mypy in CI on every project you build.
  4. Typed code is self-documenting and catches entire bug classes.

Q17 HARD
Never unreachable code.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in typing by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Never unreachable code. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Q50 BRUTAL
Typed query builder end
Your goal:

Master a core skill in typing by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Typed query builder end-to-end mypy clean. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — do not use high-level stdlib shortcuts unless the question explicitly allows it.
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Code must pass mypy on public functions without Any abuse.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Fully Typed Data Pipeline With mypy --strict
Your goal:

End-to-end typed codebase passing strict mypy — real engineering bar.

What you must do:

Build ETL pipeline: extract JSON lines, transform records, load to sqlite. Every function and class fully annotated including generics. Use TypedDict for raw records, dataclass for domain models, Protocol for Writer. PHASE 1 — Implement pipeline modules. PHASE 2 — Add pyproject.toml mypy strict config. PHASE 3 — Fix all errors until clean. PHASE 4 — Document 5 places narrowing required isinstance checks.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • mypy --strict zero errors.
  • No untyped defs except documented ignore with reason.
  • Use TypeGuard or assert for narrowing where needed.
  • 25+ unit tests runtime (types not enforced runtime).
Done when: mypy --strict passes; pipeline loads 10k records correctly.
Q52 MEGA
Generic Repository Pattern With TypeVar
Your goal:

Generic classes preserving entity type through CRUD API.

What you must do:

T = TypeVar('T', bound=EntityProtocol). Repository[T] with get(id)->T|None, list()->list[T], save(T)->None, delete(id)->bool. MemoryRepository and SqliteRepository implementations. Service layer UserService uses Repository[User] without casts. mypy verifies wrong type Repository[Product] cannot pass User ids.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • EntityProtocol defines id: str.
  • No Any in public repository API.
  • Sqlite mappers convert row to concrete T.
  • mypy tests in comments with reveal_type optional.
Done when: mypy catches Repository[User].get(product_id) type error in test file.
Q53 MEGA
Protocol-Based Structural Typing Plugin System
Your goal:

Plugins satisfy Protocol without inheritance — typing power user pattern.

What you must do:

Define Processor Protocol with process(data: bytes)->bytes and name: str. Register processors; runner accepts Processor not concrete classes. Third-party class without subclassing Protocol works if methods match. @runtime_checkable isinstance tests. Document nominal vs structural subtyping essay in TYPES.md.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • At least 3 processor implementations.
  • Runner typed def run(p: Processor).
  • runtime_checkable isinstance test.
  • mypy validates duck-typed class passes.
Done when: Non-subclass plugin type-checks; runtime isinstance works with decorator.
Q54 MEGA
TypedDict vs Dataclass vs NamedTuple Benchmark API
Your goal:

Pick right typed container per use case with full annotations.

What you must do:

Same User model as TypedDict, dataclass(frozen), NamedTuple. CRUD API for each with conversion functions between representations. JSON serialize/deserialize typed helpers. Measure ergonomics: mypy error messages, immutability, unpacking. Recommend matrix in TYPES.md for API boundary vs domain vs rows.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • All three versions fully typed.
  • Conversion functions typed no Any.
  • TotalTypedDict with NotRequired and ReadOnly (3.11+) if available documented.
  • 15+ round-trip tests.
Done when: Conversions lossless on test data; written matrix covers 6 scenarios.
Q55 MEGA
Overload Dispatch for JSON decode
Your goal:

@overload for return type depends on literal first argument.

What you must do:

decode_json(raw: str, as_type: Literal['dict'])->dict[str, Any]. decode_json(raw, as_type: Literal['list'])->list[Any]. Implementation single body; mypy narrows at call sites. Add Literal['str'] path returning str parsed from JSON string node. 10 call sites mypy-checked in tests_typing.py.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Use @overload decorators correctly.
  • Implementation signature compatible.
  • Call sites pass mypy without cast.
  • Invalid as_type literal caught.
Done when: reveal_type decode_json(s,'dict') is dict; runtime matches.
Q56 MEGA
Custom TypeAlias & NewType Domain Wrappers
Your goal:

Prevent primitive obsession with lightweight types.

What you must do:

UserId = NewType('UserId', str), Email = NewType('Email', str). Functions accept UserId not str; mypy rejects bare str. TypeAlias for JsonObject = dict[str, JsonValue] recursive union. Validate Email format at construction boundary function to_email(s: str)->Email. Build API layer using wrappers throughout.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • NewType not subclassable misuse documented.
  • JsonValue recursive alias typed.
  • to_email raises on invalid; return Email.
  • mypy rejects UserId where Email expected.
Done when: mypy errors on mixing UserId and Email; runtime validation works.
Q57 MEGA
ParamSpec & TypeVarTuple Callable Decorator
Your goal:

Advanced 3.10+ typing for decorator preserving signatures.

What you must do:

Use ParamSpec P to type decorator preserving wrapped callable signature. Apply to log_calls and timing decorators. Bonus TypeVarTuple for tuple unpacking wrapper if 3.11+. mypy verifies decorated function params unchanged at call site. Compare to untyped decorator Any leakage.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ParamSpec used in decorator return Callable[P, R].
  • Works on *args **kwargs functions.
  • Before/after mypy error demo in comments.
  • 10+ typed wrapper tests.
Done when: Decorated typed function still checked at call site by mypy.
Q58 MEGA
Runtime Validation Layer With Typed Schemas
Your goal:

Bridge static types and runtime with validation library you build.

What you must do:

Schema[T] generic validates object at runtime returns T or raises. Compose: int, str, list schema, dict schema, union, optional. Parse JSON to typed dataclass using schema inference from types (simple). Mismatch raises ValidationError with JSON path. Optional integration note with pydantic comparison.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Generic Schema[T] class.
  • Error paths like $.users[2].age.
  • Works with dataclass fields reflection.
  • 20+ validation tests.
Done when: Invalid payload raises path error; valid returns typed dataclass instance.
Q59 MEGA
Gradual Typing Migration Plan on Legacy Module
Your goal:

Practice incremental typing strategy on intentionally untyped code.

What you must do:

Given legacy.py 200 lines dynamic code. Wave 1: add types to public functions only. Wave 2: replace Any with unions and TypedDict. Wave 3: enable mypy strict on module. Track error count per wave in MIGRATION.md. Use TYPE_CHECKING for circular imports.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Provide legacy.py starter in fixtures.
  • MIGRATION.md table wave vs error count.
  • No behavior change all tests pass.
  • Final strict clean.
Done when: Error count drops wave to wave; final mypy strict zero on module.
Q60 MEGA
Typed Event Bus With Literal Event Names
Your goal:

Event name literals tie handlers to payload TypedDict types.

What you must do:

Events: Literal['user.created', 'user.deleted', 'order.placed']. Each event payload separate TypedDict. subscribe('user.created', handler) mypy checks handler param type. emit('user.created', payload) validates keys. Registry maps event name to payload type using overloads or generic binding.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Wrong payload type fails mypy at emit call.
  • Handler receives narrowed payload type.
  • Runtime emit validates required keys.
  • 12+ typed event tests.
Done when: mypy rejects order payload on user.created emit; runtime catches missing key.

15 — Async/Await & asyncio

Topic Map: Coroutines → Event loop → async/await → Tasks → asyncio API → Async patterns → Common bugs → vs threading

1. Sync vs Async Mental Model

SyncAsync
One thing at a time, blockingCooperative multitasking, non-blocking
Thread per connection (expensive)Thousands of coroutines on one thread
time.sleep blocks everythingawait asyncio.sleep yields control

2. Coroutines — What async def Really Creates

async def does NOT run the function immediately. It returns a coroutine object — a recipe the event loop executes, pausing at each await.

async def fetch():
    print("start")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)   # PAUSE here — event loop runs other tasks
    print("done")
    return 42

coro = fetch()     # nothing printed yet — coroutine created, not started
# asyncio.run(coro) or await coro actually drives it

3. Event Loop — The Scheduler

import asyncio

async def main():
    result = await fetch_data("...")
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())  # Python 3.7+ entry point — creates loop, runs, closes

4. Tasks and Concurrent Execution

async def main():
    task1 = asyncio.create_task(fetch("a"))  # schedule immediately
    task2 = asyncio.create_task(fetch("b"))

    results = await asyncio.gather(task1, task2)  # wait for both
    # or
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch("a"), fetch("b"), fetch("c")
    )

5. Key asyncio APIs

APIPurpose
asyncio.run(coro)Entry point
create_task(coro)Schedule coroutine as Task
gather(*coros)Wait for multiple, return list
wait_for(coro, timeout)Timeout wrapper
sleep(seconds)Non-blocking sleep
QueueAsync producer-consumer
Lock, Semaphore, EventSync primitives for async
TaskGroup (3.11+)Structured concurrency

6. async for / async with

async def read_stream():
    async for chunk in async_file_reader:
        process(chunk)

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    async with session.get(url) as resp:
        data = await resp.json()

7. Running Blocking Code in Async

loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
result = await loop.run_in_executor(
    None,              # default ThreadPoolExecutor
    blocking_function, # CPU or blocking I/O
    arg1, arg2
)

8. Common Bugs

  • Forgot await — coroutine never runs, warning: "coroutine was never awaited"
  • Blocking in async — time.sleep, sync HTTP, sync file I/O freezes entire loop
  • Shared mutable state without Lock in concurrent tasks
  • Creating loop manually — use asyncio.run() in scripts

9. When to Use Async

Good: Web servers, API clients, WebSockets, many concurrent I/O connections.
Bad: CPU-bound work (use multiprocessing), simple scripts, mostly sync libraries.

Gotchas:
  • Async is NOT parallelism — it's concurrency on one thread
  • Mixing sync and async code requires bridges (run_in_executor, asyncio.to_thread 3.9+)
  • Task cancellation is cooperative — must await cancellation points
How to think about this topic: One thread, many tasks, cooperative switching at await. When you await, you say "I'd wait for I/O — run something else meanwhile." NOT parallel CPU — that's multiprocessing. If you block the thread (time.sleep, sync requests), you freeze everything.

Timeline mental model

# Single-thread async timeline:
# Task A starts → hits await sleep → Task B runs → B awaits → A resumes
# Concurrent I/O, not parallel compute

Common blockers

  • Coroutine never awaited → must await or create_task.
  • Blocking call in async → use asyncio.sleep, aiohttp, run_in_executor.
  • Shared list without Lock → race conditions still exist in async.

Connects forward

→ Concurrency: when async vs threads vs processes. → Web: FastAPI, aiohttp patterns.

Key Takeaways:
  1. async def + await = cooperative multitasking for I/O.
  2. Event loop schedules coroutines; never block the loop.
  3. gather and create_task for concurrency.
  4. Async shines for I/O-bound, not CPU-bound workloads.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: typing, generators

Q1 HARD
async hello concurrent 3 sleeps timing.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in async by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: async hello concurrent 3 sleeps timing. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Use asyncio properly — no blocking calls in async code paths.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.

Q51 MEGA
Async HTTP Client With Connection Pool
Your goal:

aiohttp-style client using asyncio streams or httpx if allowed — prefer stdlib.

What you must do:

Build AsyncHTTPClient with max_connections semaphore, timeout per request, retry on 503. GET/POST with headers dict; parse JSON response optional. Connection pool reuses TCP connections where possible (asyncio.open_connection). Fetch 100 URLs concurrently with limit 10; report status histogram. Graceful shutdown cancels pending on SIGINT handler.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • asyncio.Semaphore limits concurrency.
  • Total time faster than sequential demo.
  • Timeout raises asyncio.TimeoutError wrapped.
  • 20+ tests with mock server or aiohttp test server.
Done when: 100 fetches complete with concurrency 10; histogram sums to 100.
Q52 MEGA
Async Producer-Consumer With asyncio.Queue
Your goal:

Backpressure and cancellation in async pipelines.

What you must do:

Producer coroutine generates jobs; N worker coroutines consume from asyncio.Queue. Poison pill shutdown; workers drain queue cleanly. Job processing simulated with asyncio.sleep random. Metrics: processed count, avg latency, queue high water mark. Cancel producer mid-run; workers finish in-flight then stop.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Queue maxsize for backpressure.
  • Worker exception logged; others continue.
  • Graceful shutdown under 5s after cancel.
  • 15+ async tests with pytest-asyncio or asyncio.run.
Done when: High water mark <= maxsize; shutdown leaves queue empty.
Q53 MEGA
Async Web Crawler With robots.txt Respect
Your goal:

BFS crawl with async fetch and politeness delay.

What you must do:

Seed URL; fetch pages async; extract same-domain links via regex or html.parser. Per-host semaphore max 2 concurrent; delay 0.5s between requests to host. Parse robots.txt Disallow simple rules. Output sitemap JSON: url, title, links count, depth. Limit max_pages 200 for demo.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • asyncio.gather or TaskGroup for fetches.
  • Visited set avoids duplicates.
  • robots simple prefix Disallow honored.
  • Respect max depth parameter.
Done when: Crawl stops at 200 pages; no disallowed paths fetched.
Q54 MEGA
Async Chat Server (TCP) With Rooms
Your goal:

asyncio streams server managing many clients.

What you must do:

Protocol: JSON lines {cmd, args}. Commands: join room, leave, msg, list_rooms, ping. Broadcast to room async; handle slow client with drain. Server tracks rooms dict[str, set[writer]]. Client REPL async sending commands.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • asyncio.start_server implementation.
  • Disconnect removes writer from room.
  • Ping/pong latency logged.
  • Integration test server+2 clients asyncio.
Done when: Message in room received by all members not other rooms.
Q55 MEGA
asyncio.gather vs TaskGroup Refactor Project
Your goal:

Structured concurrency patterns and exception propagation.

What you must do:

Implement same workload three ways: sequential await, gather, TaskGroup (3.11+). Workload: 20 tasks random fail. Compare behavior on first exception: gather return_exceptions vs TaskGroup cancel others. Write structured report with timing and exception graphs. Refactor provided callback-heavy code to async/await.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Document Python version for TaskGroup.
  • Exception propagation differences demonstrated.
  • Timing table 20 tasks.
  • Refactored code passes original integration tests.
Done when: TaskGroup cancels siblings on failure; report documents semantics clearly.
Q56 MEGA
Async LRU Cache With asyncio.Lock
Your goal:

Thread-safe patterns transposed to async locks.

What you must do:

AsyncLRUCache get/set with asyncio.Lock. async def get_or_fetch(key, fetcher coroutine) deduplicates in-flight fetches. Singleflight: concurrent get_or_fetch same key runs fetcher once. Simulate 50 coroutines requesting same slow key; verify one fetch.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • In-flight dict tracks pending futures.
  • Lock held minimal time.
  • Fetcher exception removes in-flight entry.
  • 12+ async tests.
Done when: 50 concurrent get_or_fetch call fetcher exactly once.
Q57 MEGA
Async File IO With aiofiles Pattern (stdlib executor)
Your goal:

Run blocking IO in executor without blocking event loop.

What you must do:

async_read_lines(path) using loop.run_in_executor for chunks. Pipeline async grep, async word count on large file. Compare wall time vs sync version; event loop responsiveness test (heartbeat coroutine every 0.1s not delayed). Process 100MB file.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Heartbeat max delay < 0.5s during processing.
  • Executor max_workers configurable.
  • No aiofiles unless optional compare.
  • Memory bounded chunk read.
Done when: Heartbeat timely during 100MB process; count matches sync.
Q58 MEGA
Async State Machine for Connection Lifecycle
Your goal:

Model reconnect backoff with async sleep states.

What you must do:

States: DISCONNECTED, CONNECTING, CONNECTED, RECONNECTING. async def run() transitions on events; exponential backoff on failure. Simulate flaky connection mock 30% fail. Log state timeline; max reconnect attempts. Cancel cleanly from CONNECTING wait.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • asyncio.Event for external cancel.
  • Backoff capped at 30s.
  • State diagram in docstring.
  • 10+ simulated connection tests.
Done when: Eventually connects on flaky mock; cancel stops retry loop.
Q59 MEGA
Mini asyncio Event Loop Stepper (Educational)
Your goal:

Understand loop by simulating task queue scheduling.

What you must do:

Implement simplified scheduler: Task objects, ready queue, future completion. Support await sleep(duration) via heap scheduled callbacks. Run coroutine to completion cooperative. Compare stepping output to real asyncio loop on tiny program. Document what real loop adds (IO polling, etc).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No asyncio.run inside mini loop core (use for compare only).
  • Sleep ordering correct two tasks.
  • Step debug prints task switches.
  • 200+ lines educational code.
Done when: Two-task sleep order matches asyncio reference run.
Q60 MEGA
Async Database Access Layer (aiosqlite)
Your goal:

Async context managers and transactions on sqlite.

What you must do:

Use aiosqlite or wrap sqlite3 in executor consistently. AsyncConnection with async with; async execute many inserts. Concurrent readers semaphore; single writer lock. Migration runner async applies SQL files in order. Benchmark 10k inserts concurrent vs serial.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Transactions async context manager commit/rollback.
  • Migration table tracks applied versions.
  • Writer lock prevents corruption.
  • 15+ async db tests temp file.
Done when: 10k inserts consistent count; migration idempotent second run.

16 — Threading vs Multiprocessing vs Async

Topic Map: GIL → threading → multiprocessing → asyncio comparison → concurrent.futures → Sync primitives → Choosing the right model

1. The GIL (Global Interpreter Lock)

CPython's GIL allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time per process. This means threads do NOT give CPU parallelism for pure Python compute.

  • Threads help: I/O-bound work (waiting on network, disk, sleep)
  • Threads don't help: CPU-bound pure Python (number crunching, loops)
  • Multiprocessing helps: CPU-bound — separate processes, separate GILs

2. Comparison Matrix

ModelBest forParallelismShared memoryComplexity
threadingI/O-boundConcurrent, not parallel (CPU)Yes (needs locks)Medium
multiprocessingCPU-boundTrue parallel (multi-core)IPC requiredHigh
asyncioMany I/O connectionsSingle-thread concurrentSingle thread safeMedium
concurrent.futuresBoth (pools)Thread or process poolVariesLow

3. threading Module

import threading

counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()

def increment():
    global counter
    with lock:
        counter += 1

threads = [threading.Thread(target=increment) for _ in range(100)]
for t in threads: t.start()
for t in threads: t.join()

3.1 Threading primitives

PrimitivePurpose
LockMutual exclusion
RLockReentrant lock (same thread can acquire twice)
SemaphoreLimit concurrent access (pool of N)
EventSignal between threads
ConditionWait for condition with lock
QueueThread-safe FIFO — producer/consumer

4. multiprocessing Module

from multiprocessing import Pool

def square(n):
    return n * n

if __name__ == "__main__":   # REQUIRED on Windows
    with Pool(4) as pool:
        results = pool.map(square, range(1000))

4.1 IPC mechanisms

  • Queue — pipe-based message passing
  • Pipe — bidirectional connection
  • Manager — shared dict/list (slow, proxy objects)
  • shared_memory (3.8+) — shared byte blocks

5. concurrent.futures — Unified API

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed

# I/O-bound
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) as ex:
    futures = [ex.submit(fetch, url) for url in urls]
    for f in as_completed(futures):
        print(f.result())

# CPU-bound
with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as ex:
    results = ex.map(compute, data_chunks)

6. Decision Flowchart

Is it waiting on I/O (network, disk)?
  YES → Many connections? → asyncio
      → Few/simpler?      → threading or asyncio
  NO (CPU-bound)          → multiprocessing / ProcessPoolExecutor

7. Deadlocks & Race Conditions

# Deadlock: two locks acquired in opposite order
# Fix: always acquire locks in same global order

# Race: read-modify-write without lock
# Fix: use Lock, or atomic operations, or Queue
Gotchas:
  • Missing if __name__ == "__main__" on Windows with multiprocessing
  • Lambda/pickling — ProcessPool requires picklable top-level functions
  • Daemon threads killed abruptly on main exit
  • Sharing mutable objects across processes without proper IPC
How to think about this topic: GIL = one bytecode thread at a time in CPython. Threads help I/O wait overlap, not CPU crunching. Processes = separate Python, separate GIL, true parallelism but IPC overhead. Choose: I/O many connections → async; I/O simpler → threads; CPU bound → processes.

Decision table (memorize)

WorkloadChoose
Many network waitsasyncio
Few blocking I/O callsthreading
CPU-heavy computationmultiprocessing / ProcessPoolExecutor

Common blockers

  • Threads don't speed CPU → GIL; use processes.
  • Windows multiprocessing → need if __name__ == "__main__".
  • Deadlock → always acquire locks in same order.

Connects forward

→ Production download managers, crawlers, data pipelines combine models.

Key Takeaways:
  1. GIL limits CPU threading — know when to switch to multiprocessing.
  2. concurrent.futures is the simplest starting point for both models.
  3. Async for massive I/O concurrency; processes for CPU parallelism.
  4. Always protect shared mutable state with locks or message passing.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: async

Q1 HARD
Thread race counter increment fix Lock.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in concurrency by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Thread race counter increment fix Lock. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Demonstrate correct synchronization and clean shutdown.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Q37 HARD
Parallel merge sort processes.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in concurrency by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Parallel merge sort processes. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Demonstrate correct synchronization and clean shutdown.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

parallel map interpreter eval.

Q41 HARD
Benchmark threading vs multiprocessing vs async matrix.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in concurrency by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Performance investigation: Benchmark threading vs multiprocessing vs async matrix. Use timeit or perf_counter, run warmup iterations, report min/median over ≥5 runs, and explain WHY the faster approach wins in terms of Python internals.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Print a timing comparison table with at least 3 input sizes.
  • Use asyncio properly — no blocking calls in async code paths.
  • Demonstrate correct synchronization and clean shutdown.
Done when: Printed timing table + written analysis of results.

Q50 BRUTAL
Download manager threads+async+progress+retry.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in concurrency by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Download manager threads+async+progress+retry. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Use asyncio properly — no blocking calls in async code paths.
  • Demonstrate correct synchronization and clean shutdown.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Thread Pool Web Scraper With Rate Limiting
Your goal:

Threading for I/O bound work with shared rate limiter.

What you must do:

Fetch 500 URLs using concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor max_workers=20. Global token bucket rate limit 10 req/s across threads. Thread-safe stats: success, fail, bytes downloaded. Retry 429 with Retry-After header respect. Export CSV results url, status, elapsed_ms.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Token bucket thread-safe with Lock.
  • Executor shutdown wait=True on exit.
  • 429 retry max 3 per URL.
  • Mock server or httpbin in tests.
Done when: Rate approximately 10/s; CSV has 500 rows.
Q52 MEGA
Multiprocessing Map-Reduce Word Count
Your goal:

Process pool for CPU work — bypass GIL on data processing.

What you must do:

Split large text file into chunks by byte ranges aligned to newline. Pool.map count words per chunk returning Counter dict. Reduce merge Counters in parent. Compare wall time vs single process on 50MB file. Handle empty file and unicode decode errors per chunk.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor or Pool.
  • Chunk boundaries don't split words mid-line.
  • Merge memory efficient for 100k unique words.
  • Speedup table 1 vs N workers.
Done when: Word counts match single-thread reference; speedup >1.5 on 4 cores.
Q53 MEGA
Producer-Consumer With queue.Queue Threads
Your goal:

Classic threading pattern with sentinel shutdown.

What you must do:

Multiple producer threads push items; multiple consumers process. queue.Queue maxsize=100 backpressure. Sentinel None stops consumers after producers join. Monitor thread prints queue size every second. Poison pill on error cancels pipeline configurable.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No busy waiting.
  • All threads join clean exit.
  • Consumer exception policy documented.
  • Process 100k items test.
Done when: 100k items processed; queue empty at end; no deadlock.
Q54 MEGA
Read-Write Lock Implementation & Benchmark
Your goal:

Many readers / exclusive writer synchronization.

What you must do:

Implement RWLock using threading.Condition or similar. Multiple concurrent readers OR one writer. Writer preference option to prevent starvation. Protect in-memory cache dict: reads get, writes invalidate+set. Benchmark read-heavy vs write-heavy workloads.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No concurrent write.
  • Readers block during write.
  • Writer blocks new readers when waiting optional policy.
  • Stress test 8 readers 2 writers 10s no corruption.
Done when: Cache never corrupted; read-heavy shows parallel speedup.
Q55 MEGA
Parallel Merge Sort With Process Pool
Your goal:

Divide data across processes recursive merge.

What you must do:

Sort list of 1M ints: sequential mergesort, parallel process mergesort threshold 10k. Pickle-safe merge function; temp files optional for large lists. Compare times 1,2,4 processes. Verify sorted output equals sorted() reference.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Parallel only above threshold.
  • Merge correct O(n).
  • Handle already sorted and reverse sorted.
  • if __name__ guard for spawn.
Done when: Output matches reference; 4 proc faster than 1 on 1M ints.
Q56 MEGA
Deadlock Detection Simulator
Your goal:

Resource allocation graph and cycle detection.

What you must do:

Simulate threads acquiring locks A,B,C in different orders causing deadlock. Build wait-for graph; detect cycles; report involved threads and locks. Implement lock ordering fix; verify no deadlock 1000 iterations. Optional: banker algorithm simple instance.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Detect deadlock within timeout in test.
  • Graph cycle algorithm documented.
  • Fixed ordering eliminates deadlock.
  • 10+ simulation scenarios.
Done when: Detector finds cycle in bad ordering; fixed version completes 1000 runs.
Q57 MEGA
Shared Memory Array Sum With multiprocessing.shared_memory
Your goal:

IPC without copying large buffers.

What you must do:

Create shared_memory block; numpy optional or array.array of doubles. Workers compute partial sum of segments; atomic add or gather in parent. Compare to pipe send chunk overhead on 10M elements. Cleanup shared memory unlink properly.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Use multiprocessing.shared_memory (3.8+).
  • No duplicate 10M list per process.
  • Sum matches built-in sum reference.
  • ResourceTracker cleanup verified.
Done when: Sum correct; shared memory faster than pipe for 10M.
Q58 MEGA
Thread-Local Request Context (Flask g-lite)
Your goal:

threading.local for per-request state in worker threads.

What you must do:

RequestContext using threading.local: request_id, user, start_time. Middleware sets context at request start clears at end. Logger includes request_id from local automatically. Simulate WSGI-like server thread per request 100 requests concurrent.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Context isolated between threads.
  • Leak test: local cleared after request.
  • Context manager set_request_context.
  • 15+ concurrent isolation tests.
Done when: Two threads simultaneous different request_id in logs.
Q59 MEGA
Process Supervision & Restart Worker Pool
Your goal:

Supervisor restarts crashed worker processes.

What you must do:

Pool of N worker processes reading tasks from multiprocessing.Queue. Supervisor monitors heartbeat pipe; restart dead worker with new Process. Task that triggers worker os._exit simulated crash. Ensure task eventually completes after restart. Graceful shutdown propagates to all workers.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Supervisor loop polling heartbeats.
  • Restart preserves worker count N.
  • Crash task retried or failed to dead letter queue.
  • Shutdown under 10s.
Done when: Killed worker replaced; pending tasks finish; shutdown clean.
Q60 MEGA
GIL Contention Benchmark Suite
Your goal:

Empirical understanding CPU threading vs multiprocessing in CPython.

What you must do:

Benchmark: CPU bound loop, IO bound sleep, mixed workload. Configurations: serial, ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor varying workers. Generate markdown report with charts ASCII or CSV for plotting. Explain results referencing GIL in GIL_REPORT.md.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Each workload reproducible seed.
  • At least 3 worker counts tested.
  • IO bound shows thread win; CPU bound process win documented.
  • Report >= 400 words analysis.
Done when: CPU bound threads no speedup; IO bound threads improve; report explains why.

17 — Stdlib Essentials & Advanced Python

Topic Map: collections/functools/itertools → pathlib/json/logging → subprocess → inspect/ast/dis → Metaclasses → Advanced patterns

1. collections — Beyond Built-ins

TypeUse case
dequeQueue, stack, BFS — O(1) both ends
CounterFrequency counting — most_common(n)
defaultdictAuto-create missing keys (graphs, grouping)
namedtupleLightweight immutable records
ChainMapLayered dict lookup

2. functools

from functools import lru_cache, partial, wraps, reduce, singledispatch

@lru_cache(maxsize=128)
def expensive(n): ...

greet = partial(print, "Hello")  # pre-fill arguments
greet("World")  # Hello World

@singledispatch
def process(arg): ...
@process.register
def _(arg: int): ...

3. itertools — Iterator Algebra

Know what each does AND implement from scratch in practice:

chain, islice, groupby, product, permutations, combinations,
cycle, repeat, count, tee, accumulate, pairwise (3.10+)

4. pathlib — Modern File Paths

from pathlib import Path

p = Path("data") / "files" / "report.txt"
p.exists(), p.read_text(), p.write_text("content")
list(p.parent.glob("*.csv"))
p.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

5. json, logging, subprocess

import json, logging, subprocess

data = json.loads('{"key": "value"}')
json.dumps(data, indent=2, default=str)

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info("message")

result = subprocess.run(["git", "status"], capture_output=True, text=True)

6. argparse — CLI Interfaces

import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("filename")
parser.add_argument("-v", "--verbose", action="store_true")
parser.add_argument("--count", type=int, default=1)
args = parser.parse_args()

7. inspect, ast, dis — Introspection

import inspect, ast, dis

inspect.signature(func)
inspect.getsource(func)
tree = ast.parse(source_code)
dis.dis(func)  # bytecode

8. Metaclasses

Classes that create classes. type is the default metaclass.

class Meta(type):
    def __new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace):
        # modify class before creation
        namespace['created_by'] = 'Meta'
        return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace)

class MyClass(metaclass=Meta):
    pass

Use cases: ORMs, API registration, validation frameworks. Reach for this rarely.

9. enum Module

from enum import Enum, auto

class Color(Enum):
    RED = auto()
    GREEN = auto()
    BLUE = auto()

Color.RED.name   # 'RED'
Color.RED.value  # 1

10. Security Warnings

  • pickle.loads — arbitrary code execution on untrusted data
  • eval/exec — never on user input
  • subprocess with shell=True — injection risk
  • yaml.load without SafeLoader — code execution

11. __init_subclass__ (3.6+)

class Base:
    def __init_subclass__(cls, **kwargs):
        super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)
        register(cls)  # auto-register subclasses
How to think about this topic: Don't memorize every module — know what exists so you reach for the right tool. collections/functools/itertools for data; pathlib for files; logging not print in prod; subprocess for shell commands; dataclasses/enum for structured data. Metaclasses: last resort.

Stdlib map by problem

ProblemModule
Counting, groupingcollections.Counter, defaultdict
File pathspathlib.Path
CLIargparse
Inspect codeinspect, ast, dis
Parallel tasksconcurrent.futures

Connects forward

→ You now have full Python foundation for DSA + web + tooling. → Next: LeetCode + FastAPI project.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Stdlib covers 90% of daily needs — learn collections, functools, itertools, pathlib cold.
  2. inspect/ast/dis for debugging and metaprogramming.
  3. Metaclasses are powerful but last resort — prefer decorators and __init_subclass__.
  4. Never unpickle or eval untrusted data.
